news_summary: Astronomical alignments are providing new insights into the universe, while a fortunate telescope observation captured the rare disintegration of a mysterious comet.
news_summary_fr: Les alignements astronomiques apportent un nouvel éclairage sur l'univers, tandis qu'une observation télescopique fortuite a permis de capturer la désintégration rare d'une comète mystérieuse.
news_summary_es: Las alineaciones astronómicas están aportando nuevos conocimientos sobre el universo, mientras que una afortunada observación con telescopio ha captado la inusual desintegración de un misterioso cometa.

articles.title: Science Through Shadows: How Astronomical Alignments Reveal the Universe
articles.summary: When one celestial object passes in front of another, it can cast a shadow that travels across space – and sometimes across Earth. These moments of alignment, known as eclipses, occultations, and transits, allow scientists to study distant objects in remarkable ways. By observing how light changes when an object briefly blocks another, astronomers can measure sizes and shapes, detect atmospheres, and refine the orbits of asteroids and planets.
articles.content:
When one celestial object passes in front of another, it can cast a shadow that travels across space – and sometimes across Earth. These moments of alignment, known as eclipses, occultations, and transits, allow scientists to study distant objects in remarkable ways. By observing how light changes when an object briefly blocks another, astronomers can measure sizes and shapes, detect atmospheres, and refine the orbits of asteroids and planets.

From left to right: Image of the total solar eclipse of 2024, an asteroid occulting a distant star, and an exoplanet transiting a star.

The Science Through Shadows project, funded by NASA’s Science Activation program and led by Fiske Planetarium at the University of Colorado Boulder, explores how these shadow-based events help scientists conduct astronomical research. The project has produced a series of short films that explain the science behind eclipses, occultations, and solar observations while highlighting the people who help make these discoveries possible – including students, educators, and volunteer citizen scientists.

The videos are designed for use in classrooms, libraries, planetariums, and informal learning environments, and are available free of charge in both English and Spanish. Versions are available in 2D formats for streaming and classroom use, as well as fulldome formats for planetariums worldwide.

Explore the seven films currently available:

Focus: The annular solar eclipse of October 14, 2023

On October 14, 2023, observers across North America experienced an annular solar eclipse, sometimes called a “ring of fire.” During an annular eclipse, the Moon passes directly in front of the Sun but appears slightly smaller in the sky, leaving a bright ring of sunlight visible around its edges.

This video explains how annular eclipses differ from total solar eclipses, explores the science behind these events, and highlights safe viewing practices. It also helps viewers understand what makes eclipse observations both scientifically valuable and deeply memorable experiences.

Focus: The total solar eclipse of April 8, 2024

A total solar eclipse is one of the most dramatic astronomical events visible from Earth. On April 8, 2024, millions of people across North America had the opportunity to witness the Moon completely block the Sun, revealing the Sun’s faint outer atmosphere, known as the corona.

This video explores what happens during a total solar eclipse, why traveling to the path of totality offers a dramatically different experience, and how scientists use eclipses to study the Sun’s atmosphere.

Focus: The science behind eclipses

Why don’t eclipses happen every month? What conditions must occur for the Sun, Earth, and Moon to align?

This episode explains the orbital mechanics that produce eclipses and clarifies the differences between solar and lunar eclipses. By addressing common misconceptions, it helps viewers understand the celestial alignments that create these spectacular events.

Focus: The Lucy occultation campaign

When an asteroid passes in front of a distant star, it briefly blocks the star’s light, casting a shadow across Earth. Astronomers call this event an occultation, and it can reveal valuable information about the asteroid’s size, shape, and surrounding environment.

This video follows the Lucy Occultation Project, where scientists and citizen scientists worked together to observe the Trojan asteroid Polymele ahead of NASA’s Lucy mission flyby. On February 3, 2023, more than 100 telescopes across two continents were deployed to capture the moment Polymele passed in front of a star. The resulting observations help scientists better understand the asteroid before the spacecraft’s encounter.

Focus: NASA’s Parker Solar Probe

NASA’s Parker Solar Probe is helping scientists explore the Sun closer than ever before. On December 24, 2024, the spacecraft made its closest approach to the Sun, traveling more than 430,000 miles per hour – faster than any human-made object.

This video explores how Parker Solar Probe studies the Sun’s outer atmosphere and helps scientists investigate long-standing questions about the solar corona and solar wind.

Focus: NASA’s PUNCH mission

NASA’s PUNCH (Polarimeter to Unify the Corona and Heliosphere) mission provides a new way to observe how the Sun influences space throughout the inner solar system.

Consisting of four suitcase-sized satellites in low-Earth orbit, PUNCH creates global, three-dimensional observations of the region between the Sun and Earth. These measurements help scientists better understand how the solar wind forms and evolves, and how solar storms travel through space.

Focus: Citizen science during recent solar eclipses

Solar eclipses create powerful opportunities for collaborative scientific research. This episode follows two large participatory science projects that took place during recent North American eclipses: the Nationwide Eclipse Ballooning Project, another NASA Science Activation-funded project that’s led by Montana State University, and Citizen CATE 2024, a NASA- and National Science Foundation-supported observing campaign.

Through balloon launches, telescope observations, and hands-on engineering challenges, students, educators, and volunteers collected atmospheric and solar data that scientists are now analyzing. The episode highlights how people with curiosity and passion can contribute meaningfully to real scientific discovery.

2D versions of these videos in both English and Spanish can be found on Fiske Planetarium’s YouTube channel, and downloadable versions are available through the project’s distribution page. Fulldome masters (1K, 2K, and 4K) are also available for free download via the Fiske Productions page, allowing planetariums around the world to share these stories of discovery with their audiences.

Through projects like Science Through Shadows, NASA’s Science Activation program helps connect everyone, everywhere with NASA Science content, experts, and opportunities to participate. Whether observing an eclipse, tracking an asteroid’s shadow, or studying data from a spacecraft, these moments of alignment offer powerful opportunities to explore how the universe works – and how people everywhere can participate in the process of discovery.

Everyone, everywhere – regardless of country of origin or citizenship status – can collaborate with professional scientists, conduct cutting-edge science, and make real discoveries as a volunteer for NASA Citizen Science projects. These projects give participants the opportunity to collaborate with professional scientists, conduct cutting-edge science, and make real discoveries related to NASA’s five research divisions: Earth science, planetary science, astrophysics, biological and physical sciences, and heliophysics. Explore available projects and get started: https://science.nasa.gov/citizen-science/
articles.reasoning: The article describes a NASA-funded educational and scientific outreach project that uses astronomical shadow events (eclipses, occultations, transits) to advance scientific understanding and engage the public, including students and citizen scientists, in meaningful space research. It highlights broad educational impact, public participation in real science, and new knowledge about the solar system and Sun, fulfilling criteria for positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Science Through Shadows Project Uses Eclipses to Teach and Discover
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
When one space object blocks the light from another, it creates a shadow that can be seen from Earth or beyond. These events, called eclipses, occultations, and transits, help scientists learn about planets, stars, and asteroids.

**What happened**
NASA funded a project called Science Through Shadows, led by the Fiske Planetarium at the University of Colorado Boulder. This project made seven short educational films explaining how these shadow events help scientists study space. The films show how people like students, teachers, and volunteers work with professional scientists to observe and understand these rare moments. The videos are free and available in English and Spanish for classrooms, libraries, and planetariums.

**Impact**
These films explain complex space events in simple ways, making science accessible to many people. By involving citizen scientists and students, the project helps more people contribute to real space research. For example, volunteers helped observe an asteroid blocking a star, improving knowledge before a NASA spacecraft arrived. This hands-on participation connects everyday people with important discoveries about the Sun and the solar system.

**What's next step**
The Science Through Shadows project will continue sharing its videos and encouraging public involvement in NASA’s citizen science projects. This effort will help more people learn about space and participate in future events, such as upcoming eclipses and asteroid studies.

**One-sentence takeaway**
NASA’s Science Through Shadows project turns rare space shadow events into learning opportunities that connect scientists and the public in real discoveries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le projet Science Through Shadows de la NASA utilise les éclipses pour enseigner et découvrir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Lorsqu’un objet spatial bloque la lumière d’un autre, cela crée une ombre visible depuis la Terre ou ailleurs. Ces événements, appelés éclipses, occultations et transits, aident les scientifiques à en apprendre plus sur les planètes, les étoiles et les astéroïdes.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
La NASA a financé un projet nommé Science Through Shadows, dirigé par le Planétarium Fiske de l’Université du Colorado à Boulder. Ce projet a produit sept courts films éducatifs expliquant comment ces événements d’ombre aident les scientifiques à étudier l’espace. Les films montrent comment des étudiants, enseignants et bénévoles travaillent avec des scientifiques professionnels pour observer et comprendre ces moments rares. Les vidéos sont gratuites et disponibles en anglais et en espagnol pour les écoles, bibliothèques et planétariums.

**Impact**
Ces films expliquent des phénomènes spatiaux complexes de manière simple, rendant la science accessible à tous. En impliquant des citoyens scientifiques et des étudiants, le projet permet à plus de personnes de contribuer à de vraies recherches spatiales. Par exemple, des bénévoles ont aidé à observer un astéroïde occultant une étoile, améliorant les connaissances avant l’arrivée d’une sonde NASA. Cette participation active relie le grand public à des découvertes importantes sur le Soleil et le système solaire.

**Étape suivante**
Le projet Science Through Shadows continuera de diffuser ses vidéos et d’encourager la participation publique aux projets de science citoyenne de la NASA. Cela aidera davantage de personnes à apprendre sur l’espace et à participer à de futurs événements, comme les éclipses et les études d’astéroïdes.

**Phrase clé**
Le projet Science Through Shadows de la NASA transforme les rares événements d’ombre spatiale en opportunités d’apprentissage qui rapprochent scientifiques et grand public dans de vraies découvertes.
articles.personality_title_es: El proyecto Ciencia a través de las Sombras de la NASA usa eclipses para enseñar y descubrir
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Cuando un objeto en el espacio bloquea la luz de otro, crea una sombra que puede verse desde la Tierra o más allá. Estos eventos, llamados eclipses, ocultaciones y tránsitos, ayudan a los científicos a aprender sobre planetas, estrellas y asteroides.

**Qué pasó**
La NASA financió un proyecto llamado Ciencia a través de las Sombras, dirigido por el Planetario Fiske de la Universidad de Colorado en Boulder. Este proyecto creó siete cortos educativos que explican cómo estos eventos de sombra ayudan a los científicos a estudiar el espacio. Los videos muestran cómo estudiantes, maestros y voluntarios trabajan con científicos profesionales para observar y entender estos momentos especiales. Los videos son gratis y están disponibles en inglés y español para escuelas, bibliotecas y planetarios.

**Impacto**
Estos videos explican eventos espaciales complejos de forma sencilla, haciendo que la ciencia sea accesible para muchas personas. Al involucrar a científicos ciudadanos y estudiantes, el proyecto ayuda a más personas a contribuir a investigaciones reales del espacio. Por ejemplo, voluntarios ayudaron a observar un asteroide que bloqueó una estrella, mejorando el conocimiento antes de que llegara una nave de la NASA. Esta participación conecta a la gente común con importantes descubrimientos sobre el Sol y el sistema solar.

**Próximo paso**
El proyecto Ciencia a través de las Sombras seguirá compartiendo sus videos y promoviendo la participación pública en proyectos de ciencia ciudadana de la NASA. Esto ayudará a más personas a aprender sobre el espacio y a participar en futuros eventos, como eclipses y estudios de asteroides.

**Frase clave**
El proyecto Ciencia a través de las Sombras de la NASA convierte los raros eventos de sombra espacial en oportunidades de aprendizaje que unen a científicos y público en descubrimientos reales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting depicting a glowing ring of sunlight encircling a dark silhouetted moon, a shadowed asteroid crossing in front of a distant twinkling star, and a small planet gently passing across a radiant star’s disk, all set against a softly colored cosmic backdrop symbolizing collaboration and discovery in astronomy.

articles.title: Mysterious comet disintegration caught by telescope after lucky break
articles.summary: Catching a comet in the process of falling apart is difficult, but a coincidence let astronomers see one in more detail than ever before using the Hubble Space Telescope – and revealed a mystery
articles.content:
Catching a comet in the process of falling apart is difficult, but a coincidence let astronomers see one in more detail than ever before using the Hubble Space Telescope – and revealed a mystery

Comet K1 captured by the Hubble Space Telescope NASA, ESA, Dennis Bodewits (AU)

By a stroke of luck, we have seen a comet just days after it cracked into four pieces. This could provide a crucial window into the history of the solar system.

John Noonan at Auburn University in Alabama and his colleagues had planned to observe a different comet with the Hubble Space Telescope, but limitations to the spacecraft’s ability to turn quickly made that impossible, so they found a new target: a comet called C/2025 K1 (ATLAS). When they pointed Hubble at K1, they saw not a single comet but four fragments.

“We have seen comets break up before – we’ve seen them break up from the ground all the time – but this one wasn’t known to have broken up when we looked at it,” says Noonan. “The amount of sheer luck that came into acquiring these images cannot be overstated.”

We have never taken such clear pictures of a comet that’s just broken up before, because it is hard to predict when one will start to crack and even harder to point a space telescope at one just in time. Thanks to the high resolution of the images, the researchers managed to calculate when K1 began to fragment, about a week before the pictures were taken.

Astronomers watched K1 over three consecutive days NASA, ESA, Dennis Bodewits (AU)

Comets are made of pristine ice from the early days of solar system formation, but their exteriors are eroded over time by sunlight and other space radiation. To get at those pristine ices, which could tell us about the environment that formed the planets, we have to look under the surface, which is exactly what a fragmenting comet allows.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

When a comet breaks, the ices inside it are expected to start sublimating, turning into gas and floating off. “These really cold ices that are being exposed to heat for the first time in billions of years, and they should start sublimating really fast,” says Noonan. But that doesn’t seem to be what happened in this case – it took about two days after K1 broke up for it to brighten, which is typically seen as a sign of sunshine lighting up sublimated gas and dust.

The cause of this delay is a mystery for now, but Noonan and his colleagues are currently working to analyse the rest of their data on K1, which should both explain the delay and reveal the makeup of the comet’s insides. “We’re about to get a really fascinating look into this comet and the early solar system,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a rare and significant scientific observation of a comet breaking apart, captured in unprecedented detail by the Hubble Space Telescope. This event provides valuable insights into the early solar system's history and composition, representing a meaningful breakthrough in space science with broad interest and impact.
articles.personality_title: Hubble captures rare breakup of comet, revealing solar system clues
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Comets are icy objects from the early solar system that sometimes break apart, but catching them in the act is very hard. Scientists want to study these breakups to learn about the solar system’s beginnings.

**What happened** – Astronomers aimed the Hubble Space Telescope at comet C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) after their first target was unavailable. To their surprise, they found K1 had recently split into four pieces. This was just days after the breakup, giving them an unusual chance to observe it closely.

**Impact** – These clear images are the best ever taken of a comet just after breaking up. The breakup exposes fresh ice from inside the comet, which has been untouched for billions of years. Studying this ice can help scientists understand how the solar system formed. Interestingly, the comet took two days to brighten after breaking apart, which is unusual and still a mystery.

**What’s next step** – Researchers are analyzing more data from Hubble to figure out why the comet’s brightness delayed and to learn more about its internal makeup. This could provide new details about comet composition and the early solar system environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A lucky Hubble observation of a comet breaking into pieces offers a rare glimpse into the solar system’s ancient past and puzzles scientists with unexpected behavior.
articles.personality_title_fr: Hubble capture la fragmentation rare d’une comète et révèle des indices sur le système solaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les comètes sont des objets glacés venant des débuts du système solaire qui se brisent parfois, mais les observer en train de se casser est très difficile. Les scientifiques veulent étudier ces ruptures pour mieux comprendre les origines du système solaire.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les astronomes ont pointé le télescope spatial Hubble vers la comète C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) après que leur première cible soit devenue inaccessible. À leur surprise, ils ont découvert que K1 s’était récemment divisée en quatre morceaux, seulement quelques jours après la cassure, ce qui leur a donné une chance rare de l’observer de près.

**Impact** – Ces images claires sont les meilleures jamais prises d’une comète juste après sa fragmentation. La rupture expose de la glace fraîche à l’intérieur, restée intacte pendant des milliards d’années. Étudier cette glace aide à comprendre la formation du système solaire. Fait intéressant, la comète a mis deux jours avant de s’éclaircir, ce qui est inhabituel et reste un mystère.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs analysent d’autres données de Hubble pour comprendre le retard de luminosité et mieux connaître la composition interne de la comète. Cela pourrait révéler de nouveaux détails sur la composition des comètes et l’environnement du système solaire primitif.

**Phrase-clé** – Une observation chanceuse de Hubble d’une comète se brisant offre un aperçu rare du passé ancien du système solaire et intrigue les scientifiques par un comportement inattendu.
articles.personality_title_es: Hubble captura la rara fragmentación de un cometa y revela pistas del sistema solar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los cometas son objetos helados del sistema solar temprano que a veces se rompen, pero es muy difícil verlos en el momento exacto. Los científicos quieren estudiar estas rupturas para entender mejor los orígenes del sistema solar.

**Qué pasó** – Los astrónomos apuntaron el telescopio espacial Hubble al cometa C/2025 K1 (ATLAS) después de que su primer objetivo no estuviera disponible. Para su sorpresa, encontraron que K1 se había partido en cuatro fragmentos hace pocos días, dándoles una oportunidad poco común de observarlo de cerca.

**Impacto** – Estas imágenes claras son las mejores que se han tomado de un cometa justo después de romperse. La ruptura muestra hielo fresco en el interior, que ha estado intacto por miles de millones de años. Estudiar este hielo ayuda a entender cómo se formó el sistema solar. Curiosamente, el cometa tardó dos días en volverse más brillante, lo que es extraño y aún es un misterio.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están analizando más datos de Hubble para explicar el retraso en el brillo y conocer mejor la composición interna del cometa. Esto podría dar nueva información sobre la composición de los cometas y el ambiente del sistema solar temprano.

**Frase clave** – Una observación afortunada de Hubble de un cometa que se parte en pedazos ofrece una mirada rara al pasado antiguo del sistema solar y plantea preguntas inesperadas a los científicos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing comet breaking apart into four luminous fragments against a deep space backdrop, each fragment surrounded by soft, shimmering icy vapor rising slowly, symbolizing the unveiling of ancient, pristine ices; the scene conveys a rare cosmic moment of discovery and mystery, with gentle natural blues, whites, and subtle golds highlighting the comet’s fragile beauty and the quiet vastness of the early solar system.
